title: Identity-Aware Proxy Access Anomalies
app: Google IAP, `Splunk_TA_microsoft-cloudservices` (Azure AD App Proxy), `Splunk_TA_zscaler` (ZPA), Cloudflare App for Splunk (Splunkbase 4501), Netskope Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 3808)
spl:
index=zt sourcetype="zscaler:zpa" earliest=-30d
| eval day=strftime(_time,"%Y-%m-%d")
| stats dc(application) as apps_today by user, day
| eventstats avg(apps_today) as baseline by user
| where apps_today > baseline*3 AND apps_today>5
| table user, day, apps_today, baseline
description: Baselines per-user access to internal apps via IAP/ZTNA; flags new apps, odd hours, or geos.
value: Security analysts detect anomalous access patterns through identity-aware proxies — users accessing more applications than peers, accessing from multiple countries, or operating outside normal hours — as indicators of credential compromise or insider threat.
implementation: Adapt to Google IAP JSON logs. Whitelist break-glass accounts.
visualization: Table (anomalies), Line chart (apps accessed per user), Heatmap (user × app).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication
  by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src span=1h
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) logs from Google BeyondCorp, Cloudflare Access, or Zscaler ZPA. Data in `index=ztna`. Cloudflare Access: `sourcetype=cloudflare:access`. Key fields: `user_email`, `app_name`, `allowed` (true/false), `ip_address`, `country`, `device_posture`, `session_duration`.
• IAP authenticates every request, not just the initial connection. Anomalies include: access from unusual locations, unusual times, accessing applications outside the user's normal pattern, or rapid application switching that suggests automated reconnaissance.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify IAP data:
```spl
index=ztna sourcetype="cloudflare:access" earliest=-24h
| stats count by allowed
```

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — Access pattern anomalies:
```spl
index=ztna earliest=-24h
| stats dc(app_name) as apps_accessed dc(country) as countries count as requests earliest(_time) as first_access latest(_time) as last_access by user_email
| eventstats avg(apps_accessed) as avg_apps stdev(apps_accessed) as stdev_apps
| eval app_zscore=round((apps_accessed - avg_apps) / if(stdev_apps>0, stdev_apps, 1), 2)
| where app_zscore > 2 OR countries > 1
| sort - app_zscore
```

Understanding this SPL: Flags users who access significantly more applications than their peers or access from multiple countries in one day. An unusually high application count from one user suggests either a compromised account (attacker enumerating accessible resources) or an admin doing legitimate work. Multi-country access in 24h is a strong indicator of credential compromise.

Off-hours access:
```spl
index=ztna earliest=-7d
| eval hour=strftime(_time, "%H")
| where hour<"06" OR hour>"22"
| stats count by user_email, app_name
| sort - count
```

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Access multiple applications from a test account. Verify the anomaly triggers.
(b) Verify normal single-app access does not trigger.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard: Row 1 — Anomalous users. Row 2 — Multi-country access. Row 3 — Off-hours access.
Alert: Immediate on multi-country access for same user.

Runbook:
1. **Multi-country** — Likely credential compromise or VPN. Force re-authentication.
2. **App enumeration** — Check if user role justifies breadth. If not, investigate.
3. **Off-hours** — Compare with on-call schedules. If unexpected, verify with user.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **Country always same** — Users may be behind a corporate proxy. Check for proxy IP.
• **No anomalies detected** — Lower the z-score threshold or extend the lookback.
grandmaExplanation: Our identity-aware proxy checks who you are every time you access an application. We look for unusual patterns — like someone suddenly accessing ten different applications when they normally use two, or logging in from two different countries on the same day — because these patterns often mean someone stole their password.
